What's fascinating about Tristan is that it's not just the silence or the distance—it’s the feeling of belonging to a place where people still depend on each other. Makes you wonder: would we really miss modern life, or just the noise that comes with it?

replies.author: @markconway707
replies.text: Hate to burst your bubble, but a bunch of this is fabricated. Just go to 16:21 to about 16:55...shows shots of several places that are not this island at all. Huge patches of monocrop corn; multiple settlements spread out and no longer shear cliffs; wide, nicely painted roads that don't exist on the island. Good documentaries do not lie like this.

replies.author: @PlanetVista4K
replies.text: It’s truly a unique place on our planet, and I’m so glad I could share this perspective with you. ❤️

comments.author: @trueunrealglobe
comments.text: We romanticize their "hand for hand" lifestyle as this beautiful, pure utopia, but it's actually an extreme survival mechanism. When you live 1,500 miles from the nearest human being, forced collectivism isn't a wholesome choice—it’s a mandatory contract where you can never truly be an individual.

replies.author: @PlanetVista4K
replies.text: That's a very insightful perspective on the realities of life on Tristan da Cunha. It definitely adds another layer to understanding their unique society.

comments.author: @pshearduk
comments.text: "The Protected Ocean" around TDC closed to fishing and mining hey.. well read this and watch the video for some fascinating but sad insights..  "Dark fishing boats" are vessels that turn off their location tracking (AIS) to avoid monitoring, often engaging in illegal, unreported, or unregulated (IUU) fishing. Around the remote island of Tristan da Cunha, these vessels pose a severe threat to the island's sustainable lobster fishery and massive Marine Protected Area.  https://youtu.be/2tuS1LLOcsI?si=7mlXF-KEBwYPkL3e

replies.author: @PlanetVista4K
replies.text: Thanks for sharing this, it's a critical issue for the island.

comments.author: @LK-uk4bl
comments.text: Where do they get electricity and fresh water? Who pays the doctors and teachers?

replies.author: @PlanetVista4K
replies.text: Those are excellent questions that highlight the unique challenges and self-sufficiency of Tristan da Cunha! I delve into some of those logistical aspects in the full documentary, but generally, they utilize a combination of generators and some renewable sources for electricity, and collect rainwater for fresh water. The community itself, with support from the UK, manages many of these essential services.

comments.author: @chuckmyntti
comments.text: why are you including images from elsewhere?  Just stretching this out?

replies.author: @PlanetVista4K
replies.text: Thanks for your feedback. It wasn't meant to stretch the video's length. Because footage of Tristan da Cunha is quite limited, some shots were used as illustrative visuals. We'll do our best to use footage that matches the location more closely in future videos.

comments.text: 10 minutes in and we're still yappin' about no runways, only by ship with the same boat clips over and over. I want to see what it's like to live there. ❤

replies.author: @RemoteEarthJourneys
replies.text: Fair point 😄. I think that's the strange balance with places like Tristan—its isolation is the story, but the everyday life is what we're all curious about. I'd honestly love to see more of the small things: what people cook, how kids spend their afternoons, or what a Saturday night at the pub feels like. What part of island life would you want to see most?

comments.author: @ChanelNatureTravel4K
comments.text: What impressed me most was how such a small community has managed to thrive in complete isolation for generations. With no airport, limited supplies, and thousands of kilometers of ocean in every direction, daily life here depends on resilience, cooperation, and self-sufficiency. It's a fascinating reminder that people can adapt and build meaningful lives almost anywhere on Earth.

replies.author: @PlanetVista4K
replies.text: You've hit the nail on the head! Their resilience, cooperation, and self-sufficiency truly are inspiring and a testament to the human spirit.

comments.author: @JesusTorres-qr1gz
comments.text: Based on your video that island has plenty of land to build a runway for large aircraft, the question is what about the winds,? Obviously the cost would be very high but remember a go fund me and many other countries can help to accomplish it.

replies.author: @timothykelly7974
replies.text: @P​⁠The Royal navy helped a lot during and after the eruption. I have been there on 3 occasions. The settlement is built on sloping land and the open area as well. Not suitable for an airstrip. I was struck by how fit, healthy, happy and welcoming the people were. The last time I was there it was calm enough to land our football team. We played against a fit , strong team. Playing downhill was a challenge as the ball rolled faster than us. Second half was better and we managed to get a draw.

replies.author: @PlanetVista4K
replies.text: That's a really insightful point about the potential for a runway and the wind considerations. It's definitely a complex issue with many factors to weigh.

comments.author: @rogeratkins7192
comments.text: Why at 28.20 is there a Queensland community (Kubin, Torres Strait, Australia) hospital in there? A long to travel!

replies.author: @PlanetVista4K
replies.text: Sharp eye — you're right, that hospital shot doesn't belong to Tristan. That's a footage slip on our end, and exactly the kind of accuracy we're working to tighten. Thanks for catching it.
